_meta.purpose: Per-segment narration scripts for the Maren walkthrough video.
_meta.audit_anchor: Each script's claims trace to specific files in the repo; see the comments inline.

01_intro:
Doctor Maren — thank you. Thank you for sharing your original technical report with us through Jim DeLong. The fact that you opened the manuscript to outside scrutiny told us, before we read a single line, that you are interested in correctness, not protection. We have tried to honor that.

What you are about to see is an audit-grade collaborative review of variational free energy minimization in active inference, organized around your Themesis Technical Report TR-2019-01v6 as a test case for the audit method itself. We treat your manuscript as a test case, not a target. We are explicit that this is a second-pass audit on work that Karl Friston has already reviewed.

The contribution we are offering is not new mathematics. The underlying identities are standard since Beal, and Friston's subsequent work. What we are offering is transparent provenance, reproducible numerical verification, and an honest map of where this manuscript is fully secured and where it most needs your expert eye.

Over the next ten minutes, we will walk through the documents, the error register, the interactive math, the active inference agents, and the open questions. Let us begin.
02_repo: The repository is one Git tree with four interlocking artifacts. The first is the manuscript itself — version two, eleven hundred and thirty-seven lines, pinned to the audit chain by sentence-level provenance. The second is the audit chain — the v1 audit of audit, five OODA worksheets running from phase one through phase five, twenty-seven documented patches v1 to v2, and a one hundred and forty-five row provenance map that traces each load-bearing claim to its evidence class and source anchor. The third is the reproducibility suite — eighty-seven pytest assertions across eleven deterministic numerical demonstrations, with bit-identical reference output and a nine-cell continuous integration matrix covering Linux, Windows, macOS, and Python three eleven through three thirteen. The fourth is this user interface, deployed at active-inference-collaborative-review.vercel.app — your tour guide for the rest.
03_docs: We start with documents. The deployed viewer surfaces every readable file in the repository — eighty files across nine logical groups. On the left, a file tree groups the manuscript, the audit chain, the OODA worksheets, the provenance trail, the reproducibility suite, and the agent code. Pick any markdown file and the viewer renders it with full KaTeX math typesetting. The manuscript v2 alone contains five hundred and eighty-three inline math expressions and twenty-two display equations, all of them rendered live. Cross-references resolve as clickable links. The provenance map is shown as a sortable, filterable table — click any header to sort, type into the box to filter, all client-side. Dark and light mode toggle from the header and persist across navigation.
04_audit:
This is the section we most need you to inspect. The audit register, accessible from the audit tab, lists all sixteen findings against your manuscript — seven Serious, seven Moderate, one Minor, none Fatal. Every single finding has been verified verbatim against your PDF in Phase 5 of the remediation plan, and every card carries a PDF-verified badge.

We want to land four findings briefly.

E1 is the role-reversal at equation 2 — the parenthetical that calls q the true posterior and p the variational density. Under the standard variational inference identity, q is variational and p is the exact posterior under the model. The audit reads this as inverted; we have flagged it as Serious with confidence five out of five.

E2a and E2b sit together in section 6.2 of the PDF, page 38. The phrase we flag — quote — "the free energy for the model is a lower bound for the free energy of the external system" — reverses the canonical bound direction. The aggravating context, which we found in Phase 5 by reading the original PDF directly, is that your own preceding paragraph on page 37 explicitly says we reverse the signs and the direction of the inequality. The bound direction in section 6.2 then goes the other way. We mark this as Serious five-of-five; this is precisely the kind of finding where a single different read from you closes it.

E10 is the CVM bridge gap — the most original of your contributions and the place where the audit explicitly says we are uncertain. We will return to it in segment eight.

E8 was the most contested finding. Phase 5 settled it by direct PDF inspection: equation B-1 on page 54 lacks the minus sign as the audit said, but the refined reading is that B-1 is the Beal-convention form and B-8 on page 58 is the Friston-convention form with the minus sign restored. Your own page 56 self-flag — quote — "we do not have agreement" — marks the same ambiguity. The settlement is on the audit register card.
05_math:
Math runner. Eleven deterministic numerical demonstrations from your perspective, runnable in the browser. The same Python source that produces the canonical reference output, ported to TypeScript and verified to match.

Test 1 is the discrete two-state model — binary eta, binary y, the master identity. Move the q slider toward 0.05 and F balloons to two point five nats. Move it to the exact posterior at q equals zero point nine one and F drops to the surprisal value of zero point three seven, the bound becoming tight. The PASS badges show that Form 1 equals Form 2 — the energy-minus-entropy and KL-plus-evidence decompositions agree to machine precision.

Test 4 is the Gaussian conjugate — closed-form KL between two Gaussians. Two sliders, one for mean and one for variance, both drive the free energy live.

Test 11 is the Complexity-Accuracy decomposition we added in Phase 2 — the third form of equation 2.5 from Parr, Pezzulo, and Friston's 2022 textbook. All three forms agree to ten decimal places on both discrete and continuous models.

The Python source is the canonical oracle. Eighty-seven pytest assertions pass on Python 3 dot 11, 3 dot 12, and 3 dot 13. Docker reproduces bit-identical output. The TypeScript port is here for interactive feedback, not for verification.
06_agents:
Active inference agents. Two reference implementations, running live in your browser, written in pure TypeScript and faithful to the variational identity. There is also a Jido scaffolding under the agents-elixir folder for production deployment under OTP, following the binding rules in AGENTS dot md.

Agent A is the discrete two-state model from Test 1. Binary eta, binary y. The agent maintains a variational posterior q-of-eta-equals-1, observes y, performs perceptual inference by exact Bayesian update, and selects an action — flip or no-flip — by minimizing expected free energy. Watch the belief converge from zero point five toward zero point nine eight as observations of y equals one accumulate. The Markov blanket partition is shown explicitly: sensory states in the obs column, active states in the action column, internal states in the belief column.

Agent B is the Gaussian conjugate from Test 4. Same architecture, continuous variational posterior. Action set negative one, zero, plus one — driving belief mean toward the homeostatic prior. m-q drifts from two toward zero; sigma-q tightens from two toward zero point three. Save and Load buttons round-trip the agent state to JSON for replay or sharing.

Neither agent is offered as a result. Both are pedagogical reference implementations of the variational identity in motion.
07_search: Search is the simplest surface. Full-text search across every readable file in the repository. Type Markov blanket — twenty-two file matches, more than a hundred total hits, ranked by frequency, each with context snippets and clickable links into the document viewer. Use it to find any term from your TR; it will surface every place that term lives in the audit chain.
08_needs_maren_intro:
We come now to the most important section. Where your expertise is most needed.

Phase 5 of the remediation plan settled the E8 finding. What remains are five Layer 2 gates on primary-source citations, and the CVM bridge proposal — together, these are the spaces where we explicitly say we are not yet sure. They are the places a single read from you changes the verdict.
08_l2_gates: The five Layer 2 gates pending: Beal 2003 section 2 dot 2 dot 1, Friston 2013 on non-equilibrium steady state, Friston 2015 equations 3 dot 1 through 3 dot 4, the Sengupta-Stemmler-Friston 2013 Helmholtz quote, and Kikuchi and Brush 1967 cluster-entropy. None of these require independent acquisition of primary sources. You know each of these literatures deeply. The gate questions are listed in Layer2 Inspection Specs dot md.
08_cvm_bridge: The CVM bridge is the place we most need your read. The audit's view is that the CVM material is promising as a proposal but not yet a derivation, because four items have not been made explicit: one, the generative model in CVM-compatible form; two, the variational family parameterized by the configuration variables; three, the objective relationship between F-CVM and F-of-q — equal, upper bound, gap controlled; and four, the assumptions under which the bridge holds. This is not a gotcha finding. If you have already constructed this bridge in unpublished notes or in literature we missed, we want to know — and the audit gets repaired in your favor.
08_friston_context: And the framing for all of this. Your TR carries a verbatim acknowledgment to Karl Friston for, quote, careful, detailed, and thoughtful reviews. Your manuscript was Friston-reviewed before publication. What we are doing is a second structured perspective, not a first opinion on unreviewed material. Phase 5 of our plan was a worked example of how the audit corrects itself: when you sent the original PDF, we found and reversed our own prior error about the arXiv identifier, which we had previously marked as suspicious. The PDF showed the watermark was real all along. We documented the reversal publicly, in the same OODA worksheet. That is the posture: repair-friendly, not defensive.
08_questions: Appendix F of the manuscript closes with ten suggested review questions. We will not read them now — they are on screen. The single question we most want your reaction to is question seven, on CVM, and the broader question ten, on whether the manuscript's overall posture of humility and provenance discipline is appropriate, excessive, or insufficient.
09_question:
Doctor Maren — we are aware that you have built much of the world we are now standing in. The variational identity you teach. The active inference framing you have helped formalize. The CVM proposal you opened to the field. These are not subjects we approach lightly. We have tried to be careful with your manuscript. We do not assume we have been careful enough.

The single open question we would most love your reaction to is this. Of the three findings we marked Serious — the q-p reversal, the bound-direction wording in section 6 dot 2, and the CVM bridge gap — which, if any, do you read differently than we do? If even one is a misreading on our part, the audit gets repaired in your favor. We will publish the reversal in the same way we reversed our prior arXiv identifier claim when you sent us the PDF.
09_thanks:
Thank you for the opportunity. Thank you for the work you have already done. We are grateful to be in the room.

A second, explicit thank-you to Jim DeLong — for the introduction, the encouragement, and for being the bridge that made this collaboration possible.

Reach back any way that suits you. Through Jim DeLong, by email, by GitHub issue, or by reply to whichever channel brought this to you.
